note: Load-bearing claims per chapter. Claim text is quoted or closely paraphrased from the chapter HTML under book/chapters/.

claims.text: The procrastinate glossary defines a job as 'the launching of a specific task with specific values for the keyword arguments.'
Row locking (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) guarantees that when a worker starts executing a job, it's the only one; two workers can't grab the same row.
Workers LISTEN for a Postgres notification sent when a job is deferred, and also poll on a timer as a backstop in case a notification is missed.
Job statuses: todo -> doing -> succeeded/failed/aborted; todo -> cancelled; a failed job whose retry policy allows another attempt goes back to todo.
Procrastinate requires Python 3.10+ and PostgreSQL 13+.
There are five connector classes; PsycopgConnector (async, psycopg 3) and SyncPsycopgConnector are the psycopg3 pair; workers require an async connector.
Connectors accept a conninfo DSN, keyword arguments, or fall back to standard libpq environment variables (PGHOST etc.).
The procrastinate CLI cannot import your app module from the current directory without PYTHONPATH=. — the official quickstart itself documents 'export PYTHONPATH=.'
The CLI reads a PROCRASTINATE_APP environment variable as an alternative to --app.

If the module defining the app is run as __main__ and tasks in another module import it, 'you will end up with two distinct instances of your app'; a task's default name is derived from the module it was defined in (e.g. __main__.greet vs examples.hello_world.greet).
'Each sync task runs in its own thread'; all async tasks run in the same event loop.
defer_async() returns the new job's integer id.
Don't batch-defer two jobs sharing the same queueing lock: the AlreadyEnqueued error rolls back the entire batch.
Task arguments are serialized with json.dumps at defer time; passing a datetime.date raises TypeError at defer time, not at execution time.
A blueprint task registered with app.add_tasks_from(bp, namespace='reports') gets the name '<namespace>:<its original dotted name>' (colon separator).
Worker concurrency defaults to 1; setting it higher runs that many async sub-workers on one event loop.
fetch_job_polling_interval defaults to 5.0 seconds in procrastinate 3.9.0.

The LISTEN/NOTIFY channel uses a dedicated database connection per worker on top of the pool; the pool default for PsycopgConnector is psycopg_pool's min_size=4, which procrastinate does not override; listen_notify=False disables the notification channel.
The worker's name= parameter labels its log lines only; the database registration row (procrastinate_workers) carries only an id and a heartbeat, not the name.
run_worker() starts an event loop, opens the app and closes both when done; run_worker_async() expects the app to already be open.
@app.task(retry=5) retries five times, so six executions in total; retry=True retries forever.
RetryStrategy's max_attempts counts retries, not executions: max_attempts=3 allows four executions; the source docstring reads 'The maximum number of attempts the job should be retried.'
RetryStrategy takes wait, linear_wait, exponential_wait and retry_exceptions (a set of exception types worth retrying).
A custom BaseRetryStrategy implements get_retry_decision() returning None or a RetryDecision, which can set retry_in/retry_at and also change the job's priority, queue, or lock.
schedule_at requires a timezone-aware datetime; a naive datetime is rejected with ValueError 'Timezone aware datetime is required' in 3.9.0.
'If a job is configured with a date in the future, it will run at the first opportunity after that date.'
Procrastinate periodic tasks support an optional sixth cron column for seconds, and the seconds column is the LAST (sixth) position, not the first.
Every periodic task must accept a timestamp argument: the Unix timestamp of the moment the run was scheduled for.

Periodic deduplication across workers is enforced by a unique constraint on (task_name, periodic_id, defer_timestamp) in the procrastinate_periodic_defers table; there is no separate scheduler process — each worker runs a periodic deferrer.
On startup a worker catches up on missed periodic schedules, but 'a task that is more than 10 minutes late will not be deferred' (configurable through the App's periodic_defaults).
Jobs sharing a lock execute serially: 'the second task cannot run before the first one has ended (successfully or not)'; among waiting locked jobs, higher priority goes first, then older ones.

Finishing a job does not notify workers that a lock has been released, so the next job on that lock waits for the worker's next database poll — with the default 5s fetch_job_polling_interval, two half-second locked jobs took ~5.6s total; with 0.2s polling, ~1.2s.
A queueing lock allows at most one job with that lock in todo status (doing does not count); deferring a second raises AlreadyEnqueued; a job can only take a single lock so no deadlock is possible.

Priority is an integer defaulting to 0, larger wins, and only orders available jobs — a worker won't idle waiting for a locked high-priority job; the docs warn a low-priority job 'could potentially take months to execute' under constant load.
pass_context=True makes the task's first argument a JobContext carrying the job row, the worker's name and queues, additional_context, and a should_abort() method.

cancel_job_by_id works on todo jobs only (delete_job=True removes the row); abort=True flags a running job, and the worker learns of it via a Postgres notification backed by polling (abort_job_polling_interval, 5 seconds default in 3.9.0).
'When a job is requested to abort and that job fails, it will not be retried (regardless of the retry strategy).'

Task and worker middleware are new in procrastinate 3.9.0 specifically (older 3.x releases don't have them); the signature is (call_next, context, worker); task middleware must match the task's sync/async flavor; worker middleware is always async; chain order is worker middleware, then worker-wide task middleware, then per-task middleware, then the task.
An async task being aborted is cancelled through asyncio (CancelledError at the current await point); a sync task must cooperate by checking context.should_abort() and raising JobAborted.
Sync tasks run through asgiref.sync.sync_to_async, each on a thread from a shared pool; concurrent sync tasks get separate threads, and the GIL means this parallelizes waiting, not computation.

Calling the sync defer() on an opened async app from code running on the event loop raises RuntimeError: 'You cannot use AsyncToSync in the same thread as an async event loop - just await the async function directly.'
With a synchronous connector, 'the only thing you'll be able to do is defer tasks'; running a worker raises SyncConnectorConfigurationError.
SQLAlchemyPsycopg2Connector can share your application's SQLAlchemy engine and connection pool if you pass the engine to app.open(); otherwise it creates its own engine.
The official worker guide's FastAPI example runs the worker inside the lifespan with install_signal_handlers=False, and on shutdown cancels the worker task and awaits it with a 10-second timeout.
A worker with no queues argument listens to every queue; a job whose task name the worker cannot import fails with TaskNotFound and is not retried.
Giving each procrastinate app its own PostgreSQL schema via 'options': '-c search_path=myschema' is a documented way to have multiple Procrastinate instances share one database.

procrastinate.testing.InMemoryConnector keeps jobs in a dict (connector.jobs, job-id -> row dict with task_name, args, status); app.replace_connector() is a context manager that restores the previous connector on exit; connector.reset() clears state.
With the in-memory connector, app.run_worker(wait=False, listen_notify=False) executes everything queued synchronously in-process.
The worker option delete_jobs accepts never (default) / successful / always, removing jobs on completion.
The builtin remove_old_jobs task takes max_hours and wraps the public API app.job_manager.delete_old_jobs(nb_hours=...).

In 3.9.0, every App() constructed in a process re-registers the builtin tasks and mutates the shared task object's name, accumulating one 'builtin:' prefix per App (builtin:builtin:...) — the long-open upstream issue procrastinate-org/procrastinate#521; the task_name column is varchar(128).

Workers heartbeat every 10 seconds by default; a job is stalled when its worker hasn't heartbeated for get_stalled_jobs' seconds_since_heartbeat (default 30s); recovery is get_stalled_jobs() + retry_job(), recommended as a periodic task with a queueing lock.

Migrations ship inside the package as plain SQL files named {version}_{serial}_{pre|post}_description.sql; procrastinate schema --migrations-path prints their location; procrastinate does not track which migrations have been applied; the zero-downtime workflow applies pre scripts, deploys, applies post scripts, repeated per intermediate version.
The procrastinate_events table records per-transition timestamps with event types including deferred, started, deferred_for_retry, failed, succeeded; the docs label this API early-stage.
All procrastinate logging goes to the logger named 'procrastinate' and every record carries an 'action' extra attribute naming the event.
docker stop sends SIGTERM to the container's main process and, after a grace period, SIGKILL; ECS's StopTask issues the equivalent of docker stop, defaulting to SIGTERM.
ECS stopTimeout is the time to wait before the container is forcefully killed if it doesn't exit normally; the default is 30 seconds when unset, and the maximum on Fargate is 120 seconds.

On python:3.12-slim, installing plain procrastinate yields a worker that dies on startup with 'ImportError: no pq wrapper available' because the base dependency is pure-python psycopg and slim images lack libpq; installing psycopg[binary] fixes it.
A Dockerfile shell-form CMD would make PID 1 a shell that swallows SIGTERM; the exec-form CMD is required for the worker to receive the signal.
The procrastinate worker CLI accepts --shutdown-graceful-timeout: how long to wait for jobs to complete when shutting down before aborting them; aborted-at-shutdown tasks see AbortReason.SHUTDOWN.
